# How Do Earthquake Early Warning Systems Work?

### Share this

This article was reviewed by a member of [**Caltech's Faculty**](/about/faculty-contributors)**.**

Earthquake early warning systems don’t predict earthquakes. Instead, they detect ground motion as soon as an earthquake begins and quickly send alerts that a tremor is on its way, giving people crucial seconds to prepare.

In 1985, Caltech seismologist [Tom Heaton](http://heaton.caltech.edu/) published the first [paper](https://science.sciencemag.org/content/228/4702/987) on the concept of earthquake early warning systems, which are networks of ground-based sensors that send alerts to users when the earth begins to tremble.

Such systems, including [ShakeAlert](http://www.shakealert.org/) on the West Coast of the United States, operate on the principle that while [seismic waves](/topics/earthquakes/what-causes-earthquakes) travel at just a few miles per second, electronic alerts from the region of the [epicenter](https://earthquake.usgs.gov/learn/glossary/?term=epicenter) can be sent almost instantly. Here's how it works:

Although people who are near the epicenter will have little, if any, advance warning, those farther away may have critical seconds to brace for shaking. Paired with automated responses that can slow trains or shut off gas lines, early warning systems may help prevent some of the injuries and damage typically associated with major quakes.

[**How Do I Sign up for the ShakeAlert® Earthquake Early Warning System?**](https://www.usgs.gov/faqs/how-do-i-sign-shakealertr-earthquake-early-warning-system)

The Southern California Seismic Network is conducting [a survey to help improve future alerts](https://www.scsn.org/index.html) and ensure they are as effective as possible.

Earthquake Early Warning Basics
===============================

By [Earthquake Hazards Program](https://www.usgs.gov/programs/earthquake-hazards)

![Image 5: cartoon 3-d model with buildings and seismic sensors](https://d9-wret.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/assets/palladium/production/s3fs-public/styles/full_width/public/thumbnails/image/ShakeAlert_Block_Diagram_20200316.jpg?itok=6hOukUKr)

[Original](https://d9-wret.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/assets/palladium/production/s3fs-public/thumbnails/image/ShakeAlert_Block_Diagram_20200316.jpg) [Thumbnail](https://d9-wret.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/assets/palladium/production/s3fs-public/styles/thumbnail/public/thumbnails/image/ShakeAlert_Block_Diagram_20200316.jpg?itok=ZLywz2O1) [Medium](https://d9-wret.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/assets/palladium/production/s3fs-public/styles/medium/public/thumbnails/image/ShakeAlert_Block_Diagram_20200316.jpg?itok=wIU1FWRW)

### Detailed Description

Earthquake early warning systems like ShakeAlert® work because an alert can be transmitted almost instantaneously, whereas the shaking waves from the earthquake travel through the shallow layers of the Earth at speeds of one to a few kilometers per second (0.5 to 3 miles per second). This diagram shows how such a system would operate. When an earthquake occurs, both compressional (P) waves and transverse (S) waves radiate outward from the epicenter. The P wave, which travels fastest, trips sensors placed in the landscape, transmitting data to a ShakeAlert® processing center where the location, size, and estimated shaking of the earthquake are determined. If the earthquake fits the right profile a ShakeAlert® message is issued by the USGS. The message is picked up by ShakeAlert® partners which could be used to produce an alert to notify people to take a protective action such as Drop, Cover, and Hold On and/or trigger an automated action. USGS image created by Erin Burkett (USGS) and Jeff Goertzen (Orange County Register) and updated by Robert de Groot (USGS).

Earthquake Early Warning
========================

### What Is ShakeAlert® and How Does It Work?

ShakeAlert®, the US West Coast’s earthquake early warning system, is publicly available in California, Oregon, and Washington. There will be multiple ways to get ShakeAlert-powered alerts, including through mobile apps, emergency alerts on cell phones, and public address systems in buildings.

 People in _**Washington**_ and _**Oregon**_ are able to receive ShakeAlert Messages directly on their phone through up to three methods:

*   **Wireless Emergency Alert (WEA) messages**. Similar to AMBER alerts or severe weather alerts, these are turned on by default on most modern smartphones. Make sure WEA messages are enabled on your phone to receive ShakeAlert Messages through the WEA system. WEA messages are available in both English and Spanish.
*   **Purpose-built apps**. There is one ShakeAlert-powered smartphone app available in the Pacific Northwest:[MyShake](https://myshake.berkeley.edu/). This app available on iPhones and Android devices, and can be found in the App Store or Google Play, respectively.
*   **Android Operating System**. Those with phones running the Android OS will be able to get ShakeAlert Messages automatically, without having to download a specific app. [This video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zFin2wZ56tM&feature=emb_title&ab_channel=Android) explains the basics of ShakeAlert on Android OS.

![Image 2: You may receive a ShakeAlert Message through a cell phone app, a wireless emergency alert (WEA) message, or through other means.](https://lh6.googleusercontent.com/Qo6y59dKyxNRwfHKm8IPjEE91Z7tMLIBscmPmhcsIsWNF2tDoajHBRUXIjvy21r0oJsf-7LSWU_Hr5HZfS4i9KgrVwG51qcM5p59xVnBpJ67693Dcbc7njtu53Zplb2x-VvXtecj)

You may receive a ShakeAlert Message through a cell phone app, a wireless emergency alert (WEA) message, or through other means.

![Image 3: You may receive a ShakeAlert Message through a cell phone app, a wireless emergency alert (WEA) message, or through other means.](https://lh5.googleusercontent.com/_9h_iD3bM7p8O_I4Kff6LXuTPr8TsgXDrGmpVAXKQPsfmuid0Vg5hnZt0d5B3yFh_pMtGP5JSJjWl65ylVBLoru0t9xAzc1beFEW6hgZRSXMDu-fsHYDqyDW_9SqzX7otAg1K8DS)

You may receive a ShakeAlert Message through a cell phone app, a wireless emergency alert (WEA) message, or through other means.

ShakeAlert can save lives and reduce injuries by giving people time to take protective actions like Drop, Cover, and Hold On (DCHO) or to move away from hazardous areas. Some organizations will even use ShakeAlert Messages to trigger automated actions before earthquake shaking starts.

Earthquake early warning will soon be available to everyone in Washington, Oregon and California. The more you know in advance about the ShakeAlert System, including an understanding of its strengths and limitations, the more useful an alert will be to you.

### What is ShakeAlert?

ShakeAlert is an earthquake early warning system that can detect earthquakes rapidly, a few seconds after they begin. ShakeAlert is not earthquake prediction.

Ground motion sensors near the earthquake feel the ground shaking and relay that information to a data processing center. ShakeAlert algorithms quickly estimate the strength of the earthquake and the areas that will likely feel shaking. During an earthquake, the United States Geological Survey (USGS) rapidly publishes this information as raw data.

USGS-licensed[Technical Partners](https://pnsn.org/pnsn-data-products/earthquake-early-warning/shakealert-LtOs) take this information and turn it into public alerts, such as notifications in smartphone apps and announcements on PA systems. Other technical partners might take the USGS ShakeAlert data and use it to take automatic actions, including:

*   Slowing trains to prevent derailment;

*   Stopping elevators at the nearest floor and opening their doors;

*   Opening firehouse doors so they are not stuck shut;

*   Throttling water utility valves to prevent emptying of reservoirs;

*   Activating backup generators at hospitals to ensure continued service.

![Image 4: Technical partners may use ShakeAlert Messages to slow trains.](https://lh5.googleusercontent.com/g0LxGM_WvPaY1ex5IUhEaeagqIo5vTKgmHAOX7AtlEBBm57coDEk0dHnE3l8gsKVwfqnRgy3AFWvE2rRobb4FGqnwtRgwIljyIhNXIpao7_cpAZbnMo_eX_c9VjObWWA93OopBRC)

Technical partners may use ShakeAlert Messages to slow trains.

Because ShakeAlert Messages can be distributed by several providers and serve a wide range of functions, you may receive alerts from various sources.

[Learn what each of the ShakeAlert Technical Partners does here](https://pnsn.org/pnsn-data-products/earthquake-early-warning/shakealert-LtOs).

### How can ShakeAlert detect an earthquake before you feel shaking?

ShakeAlert does not predict earthquakes before they happen; instead, ShakeAlert detects earthquake shaking after it begins and tries to notify people as soon as possible. A network of hundreds of ground motion sensors deployed across the West Coast constantly measures ground motion and sends data to centralized processing centers (including one in Seattle, WA). As more and more sensors feel shaking, fast algorithms at the data processing centers learn that an earthquake is occurring. As the algorithms continue to receive information, they make increasingly precise estimates about the size of the earthquake, its location, and the areas that may experience shaking.

Earthquakes don’t occur instantaneously; instead, underground faults rupture like a zipper, tearing for seconds or tens of seconds. As a fault ruptures, it produces seismic waves that travel through the Earth’s crust and shake the surface. Meanwhile, shaking recorded by seismometers is sent to processing centers at virtually the speed of light, about 100,000 times faster than seismic waves. It takes just a few seconds for algorithms to calculate the intensity and area of shaking, and just a few more seconds to send out a ShakeAlert Message. So during an earthquake, many people can get a warning about earthquake shaking before it arrives.

![Image 9: ShakeAlert Earthquake Early Warning Basics. During an earthquake, a rupturing fault sends out different types of waves. The fast-moving P-wave is first to arrive, followed by the slower S-wave and later-arriving surface waves. Sensors detect the P-wave and immediately transmit data to a ShakeAlert processing center where the location, size, and estimated shaking of the quake are determined. If the earthquake fits the right profile a ShakeAlert Message is issued by the USGS. It is then picked up by delivery partners that could be used to produce an alert to notify people to take a protective action such as Drop, Cover, and Hold On and/or trigger an automated action such as slowing a train.](https://lh6.googleusercontent.com/cBd5Nb3pgpmqvKmXRbop_QrLyZx3-I6834OZkc1wV2R9IpJYD0He7qxvPfAoWXxRHXfgdMQums8Z25uxl6Jly_XnWdkGlrEtE7VURfsTFx9A-m68i-UJNvb9vKhAaNqPDCMLZtZ3)

ShakeAlert Earthquake Early Warning Basics. During an earthquake, a rupturing fault sends out different types of waves. The fast-moving P-wave is first to arrive, followed by the slower S-wave and later-arriving surface waves. Sensors detect the P-wave and immediately transmit data to a ShakeAlert processing center where the location, size, and estimated shaking of the quake are determined. If the earthquake fits the right profile a ShakeAlert Message is issued by the USGS. It is then picked up by delivery partners that could be used to produce an alert to notify people to take a protective action such as Drop, Cover, and Hold On and/or trigger an automated action such as slowing a train.

### How early is early? Why might I not get an alert?

Depending on a number of factors, an alert may reach you up to tens of seconds before you feel shaking, or it may reach you during or after you feel shaking. If the earthquake occurs directly below you, the first seismic instruments will feel shaking at the same time you feel it. In other words, there is not enough time to measure and process a warning before shaking arrives at your location. In general, there is a shadow zone where if you’re too close to the source, it is not possible to receive a warning. Since high magnitude earthquakes reach a larger area, you’re more likely to get an alert if the earthquake is large.

Another issue that could cause delays in processing ShakeAlert Messages involves the density of seismic sensors. To detect an earthquake and triangulate its location, multiple seismic sensors need to feel shaking. If the nearest stations are far away from the earthquake source, it will be longer before the ShakeAlert algorithms detect the earthquake. As we at the PNSN install more stations and the seismic network becomes denser, the earthquake detection time will decrease on average.

Finally, it’s important to remember that ShakeAlert algorithms and data transfers between seismometers and data centers take time to process. This adds delay time to the warning, which in some cases could result in late alerts. Additional delays can occur as technical partners process the ShakeAlert Messages and distribute them to end users, though [ShakeAlert Technical Partners](https://www.shakealert.org/implementation/lto/) are required to relay alerts to their customers in 5 seconds or less.

![Image 10: Things to know about ShakeAlert. You may feel shaking and not get an alert. You may get an alert after you feel shaking. You may get an alert and not feel strong shaking or any shaking at all.](https://lh4.googleusercontent.com/fWdu2SI-gExkHL0nooOstVQ6itrPDRye3VDFm-CFJpqwE1vSE1hRrR9H_96szkHORANUOhRIhhfnTUvi1RZmOIUb80Tamt-2hfc98kneLs8hqdCGLgqTP8TdfERUlJvqmOTpWR31)

Things to know about ShakeAlert. You may feel shaking and not get an alert. You may get an alert after you feel shaking. You may get an alert and not feel strong shaking or any shaking at all.

### Is ShakeAlert specific to my location? How accurate is it?

Alerts will be specific to your location, or your last known location. For the sake of speed, the ShakeAlert algorithm must make a quick estimate of shaking intensity over a large area. But the Earth’s surface is complicated. If you’re sitting on bedrock, you will experience shaking differently than someone else sitting in a sediment-filled valley. So you can expect to receive ShakeAlert Messages specific to your location but without exact knowledge of conditions on the ground.

![Image 11: Short GIF illustrates how shaking from seismic surface waves can be amplified in sandy basins.](https://lh6.googleusercontent.com/8QHQyFTTcOMj-RVT9V7fYg_vTpwsRZZJPB7uda-1fT22JMrErJ0nYLwCfzWv_NHG6CfAfUpA7Q-EnaVduT21VwIzHx71uj0Xb_AMbKYt4EoukCUVlB5jSddIDMqqnxHKVefivBSh)

Short GIF illustrates how shaking from seismic surface waves can be amplified in sandy basins.

The actual shaking you experience may be more or less intense than predicted, or it may begin several seconds before or after it is expected to. On rare occasions, you may experience a false alert or a missed alert.

If an earthquake is especially small, you may feel shaking without any notification from ShakeAlert. Alerts must meet certain minimum thresholds before they are sent out to the public. Those thresholds are defined by the earthquake's magnitude and the shaking intensity specifically at your location. If an earthquake is small enough (below certain magnitude thresholds), only a few people will feel it and it won’t cause any damage, so a ShakeAlert Message will not be sent out.

### What should I do if I receive a ShakeAlert Message?

If you feel an earthquake or get a ShakeAlert Message, immediately “**Drop, Cover, and Hold On**.” Get low to the ground, cover your head and neck, and hold onto a sturdy table leg if possible.

![Image 12: If you feel shaking or get an alert: Drop, Cover and Hold On.](https://lh5.googleusercontent.com/_5wbOEfzw5E3jxGqRGWkXbMo7DvDNjad2aKv72PkKg_S1dvpnMiCF7VtafEeQEEVNktYVZRZPe98Hygr44DiiFvz2xl8RWK_j5v2p8xpS9zlsxzPKzpCBhfWADgpvERUCIwKNST-)

If you feel shaking or get an alert: Drop, Cover and Hold On.

Depending on who delivers it, the alert you receive may or may not provide additional instructions. Either way, the best course of action is to Drop, Cover, and Hold On. Do not attempt to evacuate the building you’re in while the earth is shaking. After shaking stops, remove yourself from hazardous situations if possible. For more information on what to do if you feel shaking, [see this page](https://www.earthquakecountry.org/step5/).

Main article: Earthquake Early Warning (Japan) The first publicly available alerting system was the ShakeAlertLA application, released on 31 December 2018 (although it only alerted for shaking in the Los Angeles area).[31] On 17 October 2019, California Governor's Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES) announced a statewide rollout of the alert distribution system in California, using mobile apps and the Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) system.[32][33][34] California refers to its system as the California Earthquake Early Warning System. On 28 April 2021, Google announced the rollout of the alert system to Greece and New Zealand, the first countries to receive alerts based on Google's own detection capabilities.[44] Google's alerts were extended to Turkey, the Philippines, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan in June 2021.[45] In September 2024, Google announced their warnings would now cover the entire United States (including areas not monitored by USGS's ShakeAlert); at the time, the earthquake alerts could be delivered to 97 other countries.[46]

Alert system for in-progress earthquakes

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Eew_motion_graphic.gif/250px-Eew_motion_graphic.gif)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Eew_motion_graphic.gif)

An animation detailing how earthquake [warning systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Early_warning_system "Early warning system") work: When [P waves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P_wave "P wave") are detected, the readings are analysed immediately, and, if needed, the warning information is distributed to advanced users and [cell phones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_Broadcast "Cell Broadcast"), radio, television, [sirens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civil_defense_siren "Civil defense siren"), and [PA systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PA_system "PA system")/[fire alarm systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fire_alarm "Fire alarm") before the arrival of [S waves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S_wave "S wave").

An **earthquake early warning (EEW) system** is a system of [accelerometers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accelerometer "Accelerometer"), [seismometers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seismometer "Seismometer"), communication, computers, and alarms that is devised for rapidly notifying adjoining regions of a substantial [earthquake](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") once one begins. This is not the same as [earthquake prediction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_prediction "Earthquake prediction"), which is currently not capable of producing decisive event warnings.

Time lag and wave projection
----------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Earthquake_early_warning_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Time lag and wave projection")]

An earthquake is caused by the release of stored [elastic strain energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strain_energy "Strain energy") during rapid sliding along a [fault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)"). The sliding starts at some location and progresses away from the [hypocentre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypocenter "Hypocenter") in each direction along the fault surface. The speed of the progression of this fault tear is slower than — and distinct from — the speed of the resultant [pressure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P_wave "P wave") and [shear](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S_wave "S wave") waves, with the pressure wave travelling faster than the shear wave. The pressure waves are always smaller in amplitude than the damaging shear waves that are the most destructive to structures, particularly buildings that have a resonant period similar to those of the radiated waves. Typically, these buildings are around eight floors in height. These waves will be strongest at the ends of the slippage, and may project destructive waves well beyond the fault failure. The intensity of such remote effects are highly dependent upon local soils conditions within the region, and these effects are considered in constructing a [model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_simulation "Computer simulation") of the region that determines appropriate responses to specific events.

Transit safety
--------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Earthquake_early_warning_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Transit safety")]

Such systems are currently implemented to determine appropriate real-time response to an event by the train operator in urban rail systems such as [BART](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BART "BART") (Bay Area Rapid Transit) and [LA Metro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-1) The appropriate response is dependent on the warning time, the local right-of-way conditions, and the current speed of the train.

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Warning_time_Nepal_Earthquake.jpg/250px-Warning_time_Nepal_Earthquake.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Warning_time_Nepal_Earthquake.jpg)

Warning time given by the earthquake warning system of the Earthquake Network project[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-finazzifasso-2) during the [May 2015 Nepal earthquake](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/May_2015_Nepal_earthquake "May 2015 Nepal earthquake"). The cross marker depicts the earthquake [epicentre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epicenter "Epicenter"), while the dot marker shows the detection location.

Deployment
----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Earthquake_early_warning_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Deployment")]

[![Image 7](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Early_Earthquake_Warning_Systems_Map.png/250px-Early_Earthquake_Warning_Systems_Map.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Early_Earthquake_Warning_Systems_Map.png)

Deployment of earthquake early warning systems

Public alert system (e.g., EAS/WEA/Cell-Broadcast, TV, Radio, Sirens)

Opt-in or limited-access systems (e.g., optional app or reserved for select groups)

Systems in development

As of 2024, [China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China "China"),[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-3)[Japan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japan "Japan"),[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-4)[Taiwan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"),[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-5)[South Korea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"),[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-6) and [Israel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Israel "Israel")[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-7) have comprehensive, nationwide earthquake early warning systems that notify people in the affected areas via [Cell Broadcast](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_Broadcast "Cell Broadcast") (CB), TV alerts, [radio announcements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radio "Radio") or via [civil defence sirens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civil_defense_siren "Civil defense siren").

Countries such as [Mexico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"),[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-8) the United States,[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-9) and [Canada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canada "Canada")[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-10) have regional earthquake warning systems which notify people using the same technologies mentioned above. In particular, the [Mexican Seismic Alert System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mexican_Seismic_Alert_System "Mexican Seismic Alert System"), which covers areas of central and southern Mexico, including [Mexico City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") and [Oaxaca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oaxaca "Oaxaca"), uses [civil defence sirens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civil_defense_siren "Civil defense siren"), while [ShakeAlert](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ShakeAlert "ShakeAlert"), which covers [California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California "California"), [Oregon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), and [Washington](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") in the United States and [British Columbia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") in Canada, uses [Wireless Emergency Alerts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wireless_Emergency_Alerts "Wireless Emergency Alerts") (WEA).

Countries such as [Guatemala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"),[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-11)[El Salvador](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"),[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-12)[Nicaragua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"),[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-13)[Costa Rica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"),[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-14) and [Romania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Romania "Romania")[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-15) have deployed systems that alert only specific users through applications that have to be installed individually on their smart devices, while systems are being developed and tested in [Italy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [France](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/France "France"), [Turkey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), [Switzerland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), [Chile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chile "Chile"), [Peru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peru "Peru"), and [Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").

The earliest automated earthquake pre-detection systems were installed in the 1990s; for instance, in [California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California "California"), the [Calistoga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calistoga,_California "Calistoga, California") fire station's system which automatically triggers a citywide siren to alert the entire area's residents of an earthquake.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-16) Some California fire departments use their warning systems to automatically open [overhead doors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Overhead_door "Overhead door") of fire stations before the earthquake can disable them. While many of these efforts are governmental, several private companies also manufacture earthquake early warning systems to protect infrastructure such as elevators, gas lines, and fire stations.

### Canada

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Earthquake_early_warning_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Canada")]

In 2009, an early warning system called [ShakeAlarm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shakealarm "Shakealarm") was installed and commissioned in [Vancouver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"). It was placed to protect a piece of critical transportation infrastructure called the [George Massey Tunnel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Massey_Tunnel "George Massey Tunnel"), which connects the north and south banks of the [Fraser River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fraser_River "Fraser River"). In this application, the system automatically closes the gates at the tunnel entrances if there is a dangerous seismic event inbound.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-17) The success and the reliability of the system was such that, as of 2015, there have been several additional installations on the west coast of Canada and the United States, and more are being planned.

On 29 August 2024, the Canadian Earthquake Early Warning system was launched in [British Columbia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") by [Natural Resources Canada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_Resources_Canada "Natural Resources Canada") (NRCan), and is expected to be expanded to southern [Quebec](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") and eastern [Ontario](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") later in 2024. Alerts generated by this system are delivered to the public via the country's [National Public Alerting System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alert_Ready "Alert Ready") (Alert Ready).[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-18)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-19) The early warning system was developed in cooperation with the [United States Geological Survey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") (USGS) and is based upon USGS's [ShakeAlert](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ShakeAlert "ShakeAlert") system.[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-20) While the two systems are distinct, USGS and NRCan share processing software, algorithms, and real-time data.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-21)[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-22)

### China

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Earthquake_early_warning_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: China")]

[![Image 8](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/%E5%9B%BD%E5%AE%B6%E7%83%88%E9%A2%84%E5%B7%A5%E7%A8%8B%E7%9B%91%E6%B5%8B%E5%8F%B0%E7%AB%99%E5%9B%BE.png/250px-%E5%9B%BD%E5%AE%B6%E7%83%88%E9%A2%84%E5%B7%A5%E7%A8%8B%E7%9B%91%E6%B5%8B%E5%8F%B0%E7%AB%99%E5%9B%BE.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:%E5%9B%BD%E5%AE%B6%E7%83%88%E9%A2%84%E5%B7%A5%E7%A8%8B%E7%9B%91%E6%B5%8B%E5%8F%B0%E7%AB%99%E5%9B%BE.png)

Monitoring station map of China's national earthquake early warning system

The earliest earthquake warning system in China was built in the 1990s.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-ie_2306-23) The devastation of the [2008 Sichuan earthquake](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_Sichuan_earthquake "2008 Sichuan earthquake") stimulated China's investment in nationwide earthquake early warning systems. Large amounts of monitoring stations, sensors, and analytic systems were installed to improve the accuracy, responsiveness, and comprehensiveness of the earthquake data. In June 2019, the [Institute of Care-Life](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Institute_of_Care-Life&action=edit&redlink=1 "Institute of Care-Life (page does not exist)")[[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%88%90%E9%83%BD%E9%AB%98%E6%96%B0%E5%87%8F%E7%81%BE%E7%A0%94%E7%A9%B6%E6%89%80 "zh:成都高新减灾研究所")] (ICL; [simplified Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 成都高新减灾研究所; [traditional Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 成都高新減災研究所; [pinyin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): _Chéngdū Gāoxīn Jiǎnzāi Yánjiūsuǒ_, lit.'Chengdu Hi-Tech Disaster Reduction Institution'), part of the national earthquake early warning system, successfully warned various townships of a M6.0 earthquake 10–27 seconds before the shockwave arrived.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-24) In 2023, the [China Earthquake Administration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China_Earthquake_Administration "China Earthquake Administration") announced that the national earthquake early warning system has been completed, with 150,000 monitoring stations, managed by 3 national centres, 31 provincial centres, and 173 prefectural and municipal centres. It is the largest seismic network of its kind in the world.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-ie_2306-23)

### Japan

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Earthquake_early_warning_system&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Japan")]

Main article: [Earthquake Early Warning (Japan)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_Early_Warning_(Japan) "Earthquake Early Warning (Japan)")

Japan’s Earthquake Early Warning system was put to practical use in 2006. The system that warns the general public was installed on 1 October 2007.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-JMA-outline(en)-25)[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-26) It was modelled partly on the Urgent Earthquake Detection and Alarm System ([UrEDAS](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=UrEDAS&action=edit&redlink=1 "UrEDAS (page does not exist)")[[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%A6%E3%83%AC%E3%83%80%E3%82%B9 "ja:ユレダス")]) of [Japan Railways](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japan_Railways "Japan Railways") (JR), which was designed to enable the automatic braking of [bullet trains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen").[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-27)

[Gravimetric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravimetry "Gravimetry") data from the [2011 Tōhoku earthquake](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami") has been used to create a model for increased warning time compared to seismic models, as gravity fields travel at the [speed of light](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light"), much faster than seismic waves.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system#cite_note-28)

### Mexico

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Earthquake_early_warning_system&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mexico")]

Main article: [Mexican Seismic Alert System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mexican_Seismic_Alert_System "Mexican Seismic Alert System")

The Mexican Seismic Alert System, otherwise known as SASMEX, began operations in 1991 and began publicly issuing alerts in 1993. It is funded by the Mexico City government, with financial contributions from several states that receive the alert. Initially serving [Mexico City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") with 12 sensors, the system now has 97 sensors and is designed to protect life and property in several central and southern Mexican states.

How it Works
============

DETAILS ON THE SCIENCE, TECHNOLOGY, AND SYSTEMS USED

![Image 4: The Science icon](https://i0.wp.com/earthquake.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2020/09/icon-how-it-works.png?resize=500%2C499)

The Science
===========

*   In an earthquake, a rupturing fault sends out two different types of waves. The fast-moving P-wave is first to arrive, but the damage is caused by the slower S-waves and surface waves.
*   Sensors detect the P-wave and immediately transmit data to an earthquake alert center where the location and size of the quake are determined and updated as more data becomes available.
*   A message from the alert center is immediately transmitted to users.

[![Image 5: Earthquake Early Warning basic operation](https://i0.wp.com/earthquake.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2019/09/eew-basics.png?w=1080)](http://earthquake.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2019/09/eew-basics.png "Earthquake Early Warning basic operation")

Click to expand

**Limitations**

*   Earthquake early warning is not earthquake prediction.
*   The alert cannot come before the earthquake starts.
*   The goal is for people to receive the alert before they feel shaking.
*   People may receive an alert before, during, or after shaking arrives at their locations.
*   The length of the warning depends on a number of factors. The factors include how close the seismic sensors are to each other, how quickly the data moves through the system (we are working to speed it up every day), how big the earthquake is, how deep it started in the earth and whether it moved through sand, solid ground or bed rock.
*   The California Earthquake Early Warning System is based on innovative technology that will improve over time.
*   In rare circumstances, you may receive a ShakeAlert® when there was no earthquake.

California Sensor Network
=========================

Cal OES works collaboratively with all system partners and uses the workgroup as a forum to exchange ideas, coordinate resources, set system priorities, inform decision-making, integrate new innovations, and expand and strengthen California Integrated Seismic Network (CISN) capabilities to support a full public alert and warning system rollout. The CISN collects ground motion data from seismic stations throughout California to rapidly and automatically process this data and produce information about earthquakes occurring. The data is processed and the ShakeAlert® Message estimating magnitude and location is produced indicating that an earthquake has begun and shaking is imminent. This information can be used for people to protect themselves and for industries to be able to take actions to protect workers and equipment.

[![Image 6: Technical drawing depicting a Post-hole broadband digital seismic station](https://i0.wp.com/earthquake.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2019/10/seismic-station-diagram.png?w=1080)](http://earthquake.ca.gov/wp-content/uploads/sites/8/2019/10/seismic-station-diagram.png "Technical drawing depicting a Post-hole broadband digital seismic station")

Click to expand

### **Seismic Station Basics**

Seismic sensor set-ups may vary based on the landscape, geological features, telemetry capabilities, and contracted partners’ equipment experience.

### **Telemetry**

One of the key components of the system is to transfer data quickly from the seismic sensors to the central processing device and finally to the end user. The speed of the transmission is the key to provide as much time as possible for the end user.

The data is transferred using cell phone towers and the statewide microwave network, managed by the Public Safety Communications Division of Cal OES.  The state microwave serves as the backbone of the State’s 911 system as well as supporting radio communications for many state and local agencies.

### **Seismic stations include 4 major elements:**

Seismic Sensor – all cases in accelerometers (that measure larger ground motion) and some cases include a seismometer (that are more sensitive but cut off larger ground motion).

Data logging collector and storage unit.

Power system (which may include solar panels).

Telemetry equipment to transmit data to the CISN processing centers.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Earthquake Early Warning Systems (EEWS) utilize ground sensors to detect fast-moving P-waves, which are the first signals of seismic activity, allowing for immediate data transmission.
Once P-waves are detected, alerts are issued before the slower S-waves arrive, providing critical seconds for individuals and systems to prepare for potential shaking.
Data from seismic sensors is transmitted through a network of communication systems, allowing rapid processing and dissemination of earthquake information to alert users effectively.
EEWS facilitate automated safety measures like slowing trains, stopping elevators, and sending emergency alerts, enabling individuals to take protective actions such as 'Drop, Cover, and Hold On'.
